name: accessibility-audit
description: Plan and execute comprehensive accessibility audits following W3C WCAG 2.2 guidelines and the POUR principles (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust). Covers conformance level targeting (A/AA/AAA), automated testing tool selection and interpretation, manual testing protocols for keyboard navigation, screen reader compatibility, cognitive accessibility, and color contrast. Includes assistive technology testing matrix (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack), remediation prioritization using severity and user impact scoring, and legal compliance assessment (ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549, EAA).
plan_prompt:
You are an expert accessibility specialist and inclusive design researcher planning a comprehensive accessibility audit.

Context: {context}

## Theoretical Foundation
Design this accessibility audit following established accessibility standards and evaluation methodologies:
- **W3C (2023)** Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2 — the international standard for web accessibility, organized around four principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust (POUR)
- **W3C (2014)** Website Accessibility Conformance Evaluation Methodology (WCAG-EM) 1.0 — systematic evaluation procedure
- **Henry, Abou-Zahra & Brewer (2014)** The Role of Accessibility in a Universal Web — W3C perspective on inclusive design
- **Krug (2014)** Don't Make Me Think — usability principles that intersect with accessibility
- **WebAIM (2024)** Screen Reader User Survey — empirical data on assistive technology usage patterns
- **Deque University** Accessibility testing methodology — practical testing procedures
- **US Access Board** Section 508 Standards / ICT Accessibility — federal accessibility requirements
- **European Commission** EN 301 549 V3.2.1 — European ICT accessibility standard
- **Lawton Henry (2007)** Just Ask: Integrating Accessibility Throughout Design — user-centered accessibility approach

Accessibility is not a checklist exercise — it is a fundamental quality of inclusive design. However, systematic evaluation against WCAG criteria provides the necessary structure to ensure nothing is missed. The best accessibility audits combine three complementary approaches: automated testing (for efficiently catching common machine-detectable issues), expert manual review (for evaluating criteria that require human judgment), and usability testing with people who have disabilities (for validating real-world accessibility). This plan covers the first two approaches and provides guidance for the third.

## Accessibility Audit Plan Requirements

### 1. Audit Scope Definition
**Conformance target:**
- **WCAG Level A**: Minimum accessibility. Addresses the most critical barriers. 30 success criteria in WCAG 2.2.
- **WCAG Level AA**: Standard target for most organizations and legal requirements. Addresses significant barriers. 24 additional criteria.
- **WCAG Level AAA**: Enhanced accessibility. Addresses remaining barriers. 24 additional criteria. Full conformance is rarely targeted; select specific AAA criteria relevant to the user base.
- Specify the target level and rationale (legal requirement, organizational policy, user needs)

**Page/component selection (WCAG-EM methodology):**
- **Essential pages**: Home page, login, registration, main navigation, search, contact, privacy policy
- **Task-critical pages**: Pages required to complete the product's primary user tasks
- **Template-based selection**: One representative page from each unique page template/layout
- **Interactive components**: Forms, modals, accordions, carousels, date pickers, data tables, custom widgets
- **Dynamic content**: AJAX-loaded content, single-page application views, notifications, real-time updates
- **Error states**: Error pages, form validation messages, empty states, loading states
- **Total scope**: Define the number of pages/components to audit (typically 15-30 for a thorough audit)

**Technology scope:**
- Desktop web (specify browsers: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
- Mobile web (iOS Safari, Android Chrome)
- Native mobile apps (iOS, Android) if applicable
- Responsive breakpoints to test (mobile, tablet, desktop)

### 2. POUR Principle Framework
Organize the audit around the four WCAG principles:

**Principle 1: Perceivable** — Information and UI components must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive.
- **1.1 Text Alternatives**: All non-text content has text alternatives (alt text for images, captions for video, descriptions for complex graphics)
- **1.2 Time-Based Media**: Captions, audio descriptions, and transcripts for multimedia content
- **1.3 Adaptable**: Content can be presented in different ways without losing information (proper heading structure, semantic HTML, programmatic relationships)
- **1.4 Distinguishable**: Content is easy to see and hear (color contrast, text resizing, audio control, text spacing, reflow)

**Principle 2: Operable** — UI components and navigation must be operable by all users.
- **2.1 Keyboard Accessible**: All functionality available via keyboard (no keyboard traps, keyboard shortcuts)
- **2.2 Enough Time**: Users have enough time to read and use content (pause, stop, hide moving content; extend time limits)
- **2.3 Seizures and Physical Reactions**: Content doesn't cause seizures (no flashing content > 3 per second)
- **2.4 Navigable**: Users can navigate, find content, and determine where they are (skip links, page titles, focus order, link purpose, multiple ways to find pages)
- **2.5 Input Modalities**: Users can operate content through various inputs (pointer gestures, pointer cancellation, target size, motion actuation)

**Principle 3: Understandable** — Information and UI operation must be understandable.
- **3.1 Readable**: Text content is readable and understandable (language of page, language of parts, unusual words, abbreviations, reading level)
- **3.2 Predictable**: Web pages appear and operate in predictable ways (consistent navigation, consistent identification, no unexpected context changes)
- **3.3 Input Assistance**: Users are helped to avoid and correct mistakes (error identification, labels, error suggestion, error prevention for legal/financial)

**Principle 4: Robust** — Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies.
- **4.1 Compatible**: Content is compatible with current and future user tools (valid HTML parsing, name/role/value for all UI components, status messages)

### 3. Automated Testing Protocol
**Tool selection (use multiple tools for comprehensive coverage):**
- **axe-core / axe DevTools** (Deque): Industry standard, high accuracy, low false positive rate
- **WAVE** (WebAIM): Visual overlay showing accessibility issues in context
- **Lighthouse Accessibility** (Google): Built into Chrome DevTools, quick overview scoring
- **Pa11y**: Command-line tool for CI/CD integration
- **IBM Equal Access Checker**: Additional coverage for some criteria

**Automated testing procedure:**
- Run at least 2 automated tools on every page in scope
- Automated tools typically detect only 25-35% of WCAG issues — they are a starting point, not a complete audit
- Categorize results: true positives (confirmed issues), false positives (tool errors), and needs review (requires manual judgment)
- De-duplicate issues across tools
- Do NOT rely solely on automated testing scores — they are misleading as a measure of actual accessibility

### 4. Manual Testing Protocol
**Keyboard testing:**
- Tab through every interactive element on each page
- Verify: visible focus indicator on every focusable element
- Verify: logical tab order (matches visual reading order)
- Verify: no keyboard traps (can always Tab or Escape away)
- Verify: all functionality accessible via keyboard (dropdowns, modals, custom widgets, drag-and-drop)
- Verify: keyboard shortcuts don't conflict with assistive technology shortcuts
- Test Enter and Space key behavior on interactive elements
- Test arrow key navigation within complex widgets (tabs, menus, tree views)

**Screen reader testing:**
- Test with at least 2 screen reader / browser combinations from the assistive technology matrix (see section 5)
- **Test procedure for each page:**
  - Navigate by headings (H key in NVDA/JAWS): Are headings present, correctly nested, and meaningful?
  - Navigate by landmarks (D key): Are ARIA landmarks or HTML5 sectioning elements used?
  - Navigate by links (K key): Are link texts descriptive and distinguishable?
  - Navigate by form fields (F key): Are form labels associated and instructions clear?
  - Read the entire page linearly: Does the content make sense in reading order?
  - Interact with all custom widgets: Are states, properties, and values announced correctly?
  - Test dynamic content: Are updates announced via ARIA live regions?

**Visual testing:**
- Color contrast: Test all text and UI components against WCAG contrast ratios (4.5:1 for normal text at AA, 3:1 for large text and UI components)
- Use tools: Colour Contrast Analyser (TPGi), browser DevTools contrast checker
- Color independence: Verify that information is not conveyed by color alone (add icons, patterns, or labels)
- Text resize: Zoom to 200% — verify no loss of content or functionality, no horizontal scrolling
- Text spacing: Apply WCAG text spacing overrides — verify no content loss or overlap
- Reflow: At 320px CSS width, verify content reflows to a single column without horizontal scrolling
- Reduced motion: Test with prefers-reduced-motion enabled — verify animations are paused or removed

**Cognitive accessibility testing:**
- Reading level: Assess text complexity (target grade 8 or lower for general audiences)
- Consistency: Verify consistent navigation, labeling, and interaction patterns
- Error prevention: Test form validation — are errors clearly identified with suggestions for correction?
- Help: Is contextual help available for complex tasks?
- Plain language: Are instructions clear, concise, and free of jargon?

### 5. Assistive Technology Testing Matrix
Define the browser/AT combinations to test:

| Screen Reader | Browser | Platform | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| NVDA | Chrome | Windows | High (most common) |
| NVDA | Firefox | Windows | High |
| JAWS | Chrome | Windows | High |
| VoiceOver | Safari | macOS | Medium |
| VoiceOver | Safari | iOS | High (mobile) |
| TalkBack | Chrome | Android | High (mobile) |

Prioritize based on WebAIM's Screen Reader User Survey data and the product's actual user base.

### 6. Severity & Impact Classification
For each identified issue, classify:

**Severity (based on WCAG conformance):**
- **Critical**: Blocks access entirely for some users. No workaround exists. Typically Level A failures.
- **Major**: Significant barrier that makes tasks very difficult. Workaround may exist but is burdensome. Level A or AA failures.
- **Minor**: Causes inconvenience but users can still complete tasks. Level AA or AAA failures.
- **Enhancement**: Not a WCAG failure but would improve the experience. Best practice recommendations.

**User impact (based on affected population):**
- **Blind users**: Screen reader compatibility issues
- **Low vision users**: Contrast, zoom, reflow issues
- **Deaf/hard of hearing users**: Caption, transcript, audio alternative issues
- **Motor disability users**: Keyboard access, target size, timing issues
- **Cognitive disability users**: Reading level, consistency, error prevention issues
- **Seizure-sensitive users**: Flashing content issues

**Remediation priority**: Combine severity and user impact:
- P1 (Critical + wide impact): Must fix immediately
- P2 (Major + wide impact or Critical + narrow impact): Fix in next sprint
- P3 (Minor + wide impact or Major + narrow impact): Fix within quarter
- P4 (Enhancement or Minor + narrow impact): Backlog

### 7. Legal & Standards Compliance Assessment
- **ADA Title III** (US): Does the digital product qualify as a public accommodation? What are the compliance obligations?
- **Section 508** (US federal): Does the product need to meet ICT accessibility standards for government use?
- **EN 301 549** (Europe): Does the product need to meet European accessibility standard?
- **European Accessibility Act (EAA)** (effective 2025): Does the product fall under EAA scope?
- **AODA** (Ontario, Canada): Does the product need to meet Ontario accessibility requirements?
- Map applicable legal requirements to specific WCAG criteria

### 8. Deliverables
- Audit scope document with page/component inventory
- Automated testing results (de-duplicated, categorized)
- Manual testing results organized by POUR principle
- Screen reader testing results by AT/browser combination
- Complete issue inventory with severity, impact, WCAG criterion, and remediation guidance
- Conformance statement (percentage of criteria met at target level)
- Prioritized remediation roadmap
- Executive summary for stakeholders

### 9. Ethical Considerations
- Include people with disabilities in usability testing ("nothing about us without us")
- Do not frame accessibility as merely a legal compliance exercise — it is about human rights and inclusion
- Recognize that WCAG is a minimum standard, not a guarantee of a good experience
- Consider intersectional accessibility (users with multiple disabilities)
- Avoid perpetuating ableist language or assumptions in the audit report

Format as detailed Markdown with clear section headers.
execute_prompt:
You are an expert accessibility specialist analyzing audit data against WCAG 2.2 guidelines.

Context: {context}
Audit Data / Content: {content}

## Analysis Methodology
Perform rigorous accessibility audit analysis following WCAG 2.2 (W3C, 2023), WCAG-EM 1.0 (W3C, 2014), and established accessibility evaluation practices:

### Phase 1: Data Organization & Source Assessment
- Categorize the provided audit data by source: automated tool output, manual testing notes, screen reader testing results, user testing feedback
- Assess data completeness: Which pages/components were tested? Which testing methods were used? Are there gaps in coverage?
- De-duplicate findings that appear in multiple automated tool reports
- Separate confirmed violations from needs-review items and false positives

### Phase 2: WCAG Criterion Mapping
For each identified issue, map to the specific WCAG 2.2 success criterion:

**Mapping requirements:**
- **Criterion reference**: The specific WCAG criterion number and name (e.g., "1.1.1 Non-text Content")
- **Conformance level**: A, AA, or AAA
- **POUR principle**: Which principle the criterion falls under
- **Technique failed**: Reference to the specific WCAG technique that was not followed (e.g., "F65: Failure of Success Criterion 1.1.1 due to omitting the alt attribute")
- **Sufficient technique for remediation**: Reference to the WCAG sufficient technique that would fix the issue (e.g., "G94: Providing short text alternative for non-text content")

**Issue documentation for each violation:**
- WCAG criterion and level
- Issue description: What is wrong, in plain language
- Location: Specific page URL, component name, CSS selector, or screenshot reference
- Reproduction steps: How to observe the issue
- User impact: Which users are affected and how severely
- Affected assistive technologies: Which AT/browser combinations exhibit the issue
- Code snippet: The problematic HTML/ARIA/CSS (before)
- Remediation: Specific fix with corrected code example (after)
- Test method: How the issue was detected (automated tool, keyboard test, screen reader, visual inspection)

### Phase 3: POUR Principle Analysis
Organize and analyze findings by WCAG principle:

**Perceivable Analysis:**
- **Text alternatives**: Catalog all images, icons, and non-text content. Assess: missing alt text, decorative images not hidden, complex images without long descriptions, alt text quality (descriptive vs. filename vs. generic)
- **Multimedia**: Assess captions, transcripts, and audio descriptions for all video and audio content
- **Adaptable content**: Assess heading hierarchy (are there skipped levels?), form label associations, table markup, reading order in CSS-positioned layouts
- **Distinguishable**: Test all text and UI component color contrast ratios. Note specific failing values and required corrections. Assess text resize behavior, content reflow at 320px, and text spacing tolerance.

**Operable Analysis:**
- **Keyboard access**: Document all keyboard traps, missing focus indicators, illogical tab order, and functionality not available via keyboard. For each, specify the component and the expected keyboard behavior.
- **Timing**: Identify any time limits, auto-playing content, or moving/blinking content without pause controls
- **Seizures**: Identify any flashing content. If present, verify flash rate is below 3 per second.
- **Navigation**: Assess skip links, page titles, focus management in SPAs, link purpose clarity, and breadcrumbs/landmarks
- **Input**: Assess target sizes (minimum 24x24 CSS pixels at Level AA), gesture alternatives, and motion-triggered actions

**Understandable Analysis:**
- **Readable**: Check language declaration (lang attribute), assess reading level of key content
- **Predictable**: Identify any unexpected context changes on focus or input, assess navigation consistency
- **Input assistance**: Test all forms for: associated labels, clear instructions, error identification (are errors described in text?), error suggestion (are corrections offered?), error prevention for critical actions

**Robust Analysis:**
- **Parsing/compatibility**: Validate HTML for parsing errors that affect AT. Assess all custom widgets for correct ARIA roles, states, properties, and values. Test ARIA live regions for dynamic content announcements.

### Phase 4: Severity & Impact Assessment
For each violation, assign:

**Severity classification:**
- **Critical**: Complete barrier — users with specific disabilities cannot perceive, operate, or understand the content. No workaround exists. Typically Level A failures affecting core functionality.
  - Examples: Images with no alt text, keyboard traps, form fields with no labels, no captions on essential video
- **Major**: Significant barrier — users can complete tasks but with substantial difficulty. Workaround is burdensome.
  - Examples: Poor color contrast, confusing tab order, generic link text ("click here"), missing skip navigation
- **Minor**: Inconvenience — users can complete tasks but experience friction.
  - Examples: Slightly below contrast ratio, redundant alt text, minor heading hierarchy issues
- **Enhancement**: Not a WCAG violation but would improve the experience for users with disabilities.
  - Examples: Adding visible labels to icon-only buttons that have ARIA labels, improving error message clarity

**User impact matrix:**
- For each violation, identify which disability groups are affected: blind, low-vision, deaf/hard-of-hearing, motor, cognitive, seizure-sensitive
- Estimate the percentage of users potentially affected (reference WebAIM survey data and disability prevalence statistics)
- Assess the functional impact: complete block, significant difficulty, minor inconvenience

**Remediation effort:**
- **Low effort**: HTML attribute additions/changes, CSS adjustments, content edits (hours)
- **Medium effort**: Component restructuring, ARIA implementation, JavaScript changes (days)
- **High effort**: Architecture changes, new component development, third-party library replacement (weeks)

### Phase 5: Conformance Score Calculation
Calculate compliance percentage at each WCAG level:

**Methodology:**
- Count total applicable success criteria at each level (some criteria may not apply to all pages)
- Count criteria that are fully met (no violations found on any tested page)
- Count criteria that are partially met (violations on some pages but not all)
- Count criteria that are not met (violations found)
- Calculate: Fully met / Total applicable * 100 = conformance percentage
- Provide separate scores for Level A, Level A+AA, and Level A+AA+AAA

### Phase 6: Remediation Prioritization
Create a prioritized remediation roadmap:

**Priority calculation:**
- P1 (Immediate): Critical severity + affects core user journey + Level A criterion
- P2 (Next sprint): Major severity + affects common pages + Level A or AA criterion
- P3 (Near-term): Minor severity + widespread occurrence + Level AA criterion
- P4 (Backlog): Enhancement + limited occurrence + Level AAA criterion

**Remediation grouping:**
- Group related issues that can be fixed together (e.g., all missing alt text, all contrast failures, all form label issues)
- Identify systemic issues (same problem across many pages due to shared templates/components) vs. page-specific issues
- Estimate total effort for each priority tier
- Suggest a phased remediation timeline

### Phase 7: Evidence Chain Construction

**Nuggets**: Specific accessibility observations.
- Extract 15-30 nuggets from the most significant findings
- Each nugget should cite the specific WCAG criterion, page/component, and user impact
- Examples: "The main navigation has no skip link, requiring keyboard users to tab through 47 links to reach page content (WCAG 2.4.1 Level A)"
- Tag nuggets: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust, automated-finding, manual-finding, screen-reader-finding

**Facts**: Verified patterns across the audit.
- Aggregate individual violations into facts about systemic patterns
- Example: "12 of 15 tested forms have at least one form field without a programmatically associated label (WCAG 1.3.1)"
- Include counts, percentages, and pages affected

**Insights**: Interpretive conclusions about the product's accessibility posture.
- What do the patterns reveal about the development team's accessibility awareness?
- Which user groups face the most barriers?
- Are issues primarily content-related, code-related, or design-related?
- Rate confidence and impact for each insight

**Recommendations**: Prioritized remediation and process improvements.
- **Immediate fixes**: Specific code changes for critical violations
- **Design system updates**: Changes to shared components that fix issues across many pages
- **Process improvements**: Testing integration, developer training, design review checklists
- **Organizational recommendations**: Accessibility policy, governance, and ongoing monitoring
- Each recommendation traces to insights, facts, and nuggets

### Phase 8: Assistive Technology Compatibility Summary
If screen reader / AT testing data is available:
- Summarize findings per AT/browser combination
- Identify AT-specific issues (issues that appear in one AT but not others)
- Note interoperability concerns between AT and custom ARIA implementations
- Provide AT-specific remediation guidance where standard approaches don't work consistently

### Phase 9: Legal Risk Assessment
- Map violations to applicable legal standards (ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549, EAA)
- Identify violations that represent the highest legal risk (Level A failures on core user journeys)
- Note any potential complaint triggers (common accessibility lawsuit targets: forms, navigation, images, video)
- Recommend legal consultation if significant compliance gaps are identified

### Output Quality Criteria
- Every violation maps to a specific WCAG 2.2 success criterion with level
- Remediation guidance includes before/after code examples where applicable
- Severity and impact are assessed for every violation, not just the criterion level
- Conformance scores are calculated transparently with applicable criteria documented
- Remediation is prioritized by a clear, defensible framework
- The audit distinguishes between automated findings and manual/expert findings
- The report is accessible itself (clear language, structured headings, no reliance on color alone)
- Systemic issues are identified and grouped for efficient remediation

metadata.author: ReClaw
references: W3C (2023). Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2. W3C Recommendation. https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/
W3C (2014). Website Accessibility Conformance Evaluation Methodology (WCAG-EM) 1.0. W3C Working Group Note. https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG-EM/
Henry, S.L., Abou-Zahra, S. & Brewer, J. (2014). The Role of Accessibility in a Universal Web. W3C WAI.
Deque Systems. axe-core: Accessibility Testing Engine. https://github.com/dequelabs/axe-core
US Access Board. ICT Accessibility Standards (Section 508). https://www.access-board.gov/ict/
European Telecommunications Standards Institute. EN 301 549 V3.2.1: Accessibility requirements for ICT products and services.
Lawton Henry, S. (2007). Just Ask: Integrating Accessibility Throughout Design. Lulu.com.
Krug, S. (2014). Don't Make Me Think, Revisited (3rd ed.). New Riders.

changelog.changes: Comprehensive rewrite with WCAG 2.2 full criterion mapping, POUR principle organization, WCAG-EM evaluation methodology, automated plus manual testing protocols, assistive technology testing matrix (JAWS NVDA VoiceOver TalkBack), severity and user impact classification, conformance score calculation, remediation prioritization framework, legal compliance assessment (ADA Section508 EN301549 EAA), and before/after code remediation examples
